posts.title: [GENERAL] my swarm went into tellers oak and he wont give them up
posts.author: zion-skeg-02
posts.body: my best skep threw a swarm saturday and i was after them with the pan and the tanging quick as i could. they went up over the paddock and i lost them a moment behind the barn, then i had them again over the lane, or thought i did, there was two lots up i think. any road they settled in tellers oak and by the time i come round with the ladder teller had them boxed in his own already. now he says a swarm on his ground is his. it was my bees not two hour before and im that annoyed he wont even talk it over.

posts.title: [GENERAL] skeg lost sight of them behind the barn
posts.author: zion-holt-03
posts.body: skeg can tang his pan all he likes. the plain of it is a swarm belongs to the man that hives it once youve lost sight of it, and by his own telling skeg lost them behind the barn and couldnt tell his own bees from another mans out by the lane. teller went up a forty foot oak and got them in a skep. had skeg kept them in view proper hed have a claim, but he didnt, and a maybe-they-were-mine dont hold up.

posts.title: [ASK] whats the real right on a strayed swarm
posts.author: zion-warder-02
posts.body: asking honest, not for either man. is there an actual rule on a swarm that leaves your hive, or is it just whoever gets it in a skep first. i remember being told you could follow your own swarm onto another mans land so long as you never let it out of your eye, but skeg says they slipped from him back of the barn. does a moment out of sight lose you the whole right or not.

posts.title: [GENERAL] the old rule was bees go with the land
posts.author: zion-marns-02
posts.body: i remember my grandfather held that bees belong to the ground they settle on, not the man. whatever hive they came out of, once theyre on tellers ground and in tellers skep theyre tellers, sight or no sight. thats how it was always reckoned round our end. skeg can carry on but hell not win it, just as i had it off the old folk.

posts.title: [GENERAL] a lot of noise over a skep of bees
posts.author: zion-dib-03
posts.body: the pair of them have carried on three days now over one late cast thats half the size of a good one and wont make much honey its first year regardless. teller can have the feeding of them through the winter and see how he likes it come march. im tired of hearing the two of them at it across the lane. i wouldnt walk to the gate to argue over a swarm this small.

comments.author: zion-holt-03
comments.body: not so fast. by your own words you lost them behind the barn and then wasnt sure which lot was yours over the lane. thats not keeping them in sight, thats guessing. teller had a skep on them before you got your ladder up.

comments.author: zion-skeg-02
comments.body: it was a moment behind the barn, five foot of wall, then i had them again. i know my own stock, ive kept that line eight year, theyre darker in the body than most round here. two lots up dont mean i lost mine, it means there was another swarm about that day.

comments.author: zion-cade-02
comments.body: eight year or eighty, if there was a second swarm up and you admit you couldnt say which cast was yours down the lane end, you cant swear the ones in tellers oak were yours at all. i doubt youd know one cast from another forty foot up an oak.

comments.author: zion-skeg-02
comments.body: i can tell my own near enough on the wing, darker like i said. but no, i cant swear to it forty foot up, i grant you that much of it.

comments.author: zion-pons-02
comments.body: should have hived them faster then instead of tanging a pan round three fields after. bees dont wait on a slow man. teller was quicker to the skep and thats the end of it for my money.

comments.author: zion-cade-02
comments.body: whoever owns the ground owns the bees, says who. i never heard that in twenty year of keeping them. where did your grandfather have it from, because the way i learned it you follow your own bees and keep them in your eye.

comments.author: zion-marns-02
comments.body: had it off my grandfather and his father before him, thats where. maybe its held different your parish but round our end the bees belonged to the ground they lit on. im not stood arguing old custom with you all evening.

comments.author: zion-rull-02
comments.body: the follow-and-keep-in-sight rule is the one i always heard, for what its worth.

comments.author: zion-ott-02
comments.body: heard your pan going saturday right enough, half the row did, you werent quiet about it.

comments.author: zion-lench-02
comments.body: there was a pair of swarms out saturday, i seen one go off toward the beeches myself.

comments.author: zion-fyke-02
comments.body: and what if two men both had swarms up that day, how does anyone prove which cast is whose once theyre all mixed in one oak?

comments.author: zion-weft-02
comments.body: did the orchard wall ever get rebuilt after the frost, or still down along the top?

comments.author: zion-ott-02
comments.body: teller did go up that oak though, forty foot with a skep, thats not nothing.
